info: Northern Europe lay buried under ice, with a barren polar desert to its south and open loess steppe beyond that — a cold, treeless world shaped more by grazing megafauna than by fire.
background: Northern Europe lay under a vast ice sheet covering all of Scandinavia and most of Britain. Immediately south of the ice margin stretched a zone of polar desert — barren, wind-scoured periglacial terrain with virtually no vegetation cover, covering what is now the Netherlands, northern Germany, Belgium and southern Britain. Further south, the dominant landscape was open loess steppe and herb tundra: a cold, windswept plain dominated by grasses, Artemisia, Chenopodiaceae and a surprisingly rich forb layer including Thalictrum, Sanguisorba, Helianthemum and Armeria. This was not the uniform grass steppe it was once assumed to be — eDNA evidence shows forbs dominated over graminoids, pointing to large herbivores rather than fire as the main structuring force. Scattered microrefugia — sheltered valleys, south-facing slopes, possibly thermal springs — harboured isolated stands of Pinus and Betula. The southern fringe of the map, along the Atlantic coast of Iberia and in the foothills of the Pyrenees, Apennines and Balkans, supported open parkland and scattered deciduous woodland — the refugia from which postglacial recolonisation would later proceed. The North Sea basin (Doggerland) was largely exposed land, cold and dry, not yet the wetland it would become. Overall canopy cover across the map was minimal — perhaps 85–90% fully open, with open woodland only in southern refugial zones.
floraDescription: The Last Glacial Maximum was characterised by the vast mammoth steppe, an open landscape of herb-rich grasslands, scattered shrubs and only isolated pockets of trees. Contrary to traditional interpretations, this was not a barren polar desert but a remarkably diverse ecosystem filled with flowering plants, grasses and hardy shrubs. Large herds of mammoths, bison and wild horses continuously grazed, browsed and trampled the vegetation, preventing forests from becoming established despite local climatic conditions that could support scattered woodland. Small refuges of pine and birch survived in sheltered valleys and southern regions, ready to recolonise Europe as the climate warmed. Vegetation structure was shaped primarily by megaherbivores rather than by fire or human activity.
faunaDescription: The Last Glacial Maximum was home to one of Europe's most remarkable ecosystems: the mammoth steppe. Despite the cold climate, vast herb-rich grasslands stretched across much of northern Europe, maintained by enormous herds of grazing mammals rather than by climate alone. Woolly mammoths, rhinoceroses, bison and wild horses continuously grazed, browsed and trampled the landscape, preventing forests from taking hold. This rich herbivore community supported formidable predators such as cave lions and wolves. Rivers, wetlands and scattered woodland refuges added further diversity to an otherwise open landscape. Europe still retained much of its Ice Age ecological richness, although the gradual disappearance of its largest mammals had already begun.
